original_article:
27

AArtificial intelligence (AI) can already predict the future. Police forces are using it to map when and where crime is likely to occur. Doctors can use it to predict when a patient is most likely to have a heart attack or stroke. Researchers are even trying to give AI imagination so it can plan for unexpected consequences.

Many decisions in our lives require a good forecast, and AI is almost always better at forecasting than we are. Yet for all these technological advances, we still seem to deeply lack confidence in AI predictions. Recent cases show that people don`t like relying on AI and prefer to trust human experts, even if these experts are wrong.

If we want AI to really benefit people, we need to find a way to get people to trust it. To do that, we need to understand why people are so reluctant to trust AI in the first place.

28

BTake the case of Watson for Oncology, one of technology giant IBM`s supercomputer programs. Their attempt to promote this program to cancer doctors was a PR disaster. The AI promised to deliver top-quality recommendations on the treatment of 12 cancers that accounted for 80% of the world`s cases. But when doctors first interacted with Watson, they found themselves in a rather difficult situation. On the one hand, if Watson provided guidance about a treatment that coincided with their own opinions, physicians did not see much point in Watson`s recommendations. The supercomputer was simply telling them what they already knew, and these recommendations did not change the actual treatment.

On the other hand, if Watson generated a recommendation that contradicted the experts` opinion, doctors would typically conclude that Watson wasn`t competent. And the machine wouldn`t be able to explain why its treatment was plausible because its machine-learning algorithms were simply too complex to be fully understood by humans. Consequently, this has caused even more suspicion and disbelief, leading many doctors to ignore the seemingly outlandish AI recommendations and stick to their own expertise.

29

CThis is just one example of people`s lack of confidence in AI and their reluctance to accept what AI has to offer. Trust in other people is often based on our understanding of how others think and having experience of their reliability. This helps create a psychological feeling of safety. AI, on the other hand, is still fairly new and unfamiliar to most people. Even if it can be technically explained (and that`s not always the case), AI`s decision-making process is usually too difficult for most people to comprehend. And interacting with something we don`t understand can cause anxiety and give us a sense that we`re losing control.

Many people are also simply not familiar with many instances of AI actually working, because it often happens in the background. Instead, they are acutely aware of instances where AI goes wrong. Embarrassing AI failures receive a disproportionate amount of media attention, emphasising the message that we cannot rely on technology. Machine learning is not foolproof, in part because the humans who design it aren`t.

30

DFeelings about AI run deep. In a recent experiment, people from a range of backgrounds were given various sci-fi films about AI to watch and then asked questions about automation in everyday life. It was found that, regardless of whether the film they watched depicted AI in a positive or negative light, simply watching a cinematic vision of our technological future polarised the participants` attitudes. Optimists became more extreme in their enthusiasm for AI and sceptics became even more guarded.

This suggests people use relevant evidence about AI in a biased manner to support their existing attitudes, a deep-rooted human tendency known as "confirmation bias". As AI is represented more and more in media and entertainment, it could lead to a society split between those who benefit from AI and those who reject it. More pertinently, refusing to accept the advantages offered by AI could place a large group of people at a serious disadvantage.

31

EFortunately, we already have some ideas about how to improve trust in AI. Simply having previous experience with AI can significantly improve people`s opinions about the technology, as was found in the study mentioned above. Evidence also suggests the more you use other technologies such as the internet, the more you trust them.

Another solution may be to reveal more about the algorithms which AI uses and the purposes they serve. Several high-profile social media companies and online marketplaces already release transparency reports about government requests and surveillance disclosures. A similar practice for AI could help people have a better understanding of the way algorithmic decisions are made.

32

FResearch suggests that allowing people some control over AI decision-making could also improve trust and enable AI to learn from human experience. For example, one study showed that when people were allowed the freedom to slightly modify an algorithm, they felt more satisfied with its decisions, more likely to believe it was superior and more likely to use it in the future.

We don`t need to understand the intricate inner workings of AI systems, but if people are given a degree of responsibility for how they are implemented, they will be more willing to accept AI into their lives.
article:
27

AArtificial intelligence (AI) already functions as a prognostic apparatus, extrapolating future contingencies from vast datasets through probabilistic and inferential mechanisms. Police forces employ it to construct granular spatiotemporal maps that estimate the likelihood of criminal occurrences across specific times and locales. Medical practitioners utilize these predictive capacities to identify periods of heightened risk for patients, including the increased probability of heart attacks and strokes. Researchers are attempting to endow AI with rudimentary imaginative faculties so it can simulate novel scenarios and plan for unexpected downstream consequences.

Because numerous life decisions depend upon accurate forecasting—and because AI frequently surpasses human forecasters in precision—the appeal of integrating such systems into decision-making processes is substantial. Yet despite these demonstrable technological advances, a pronounced deficit of confidence in AI-generated forecasts persists among many stakeholders. Recent examples reveal that people often resist reliance on algorithmic outputs, preferring human experts even in circumstances where those experts may be erroneous.

For AI to yield substantive benefits, it is imperative to devise interventions that effectively cultivate public trust in these systems. Achieving that aim requires a rigorous account of the psychological, cultural and epistemic factors that engender public reluctance to place trust in AI.

28

BConsider Watson for Oncology, a prominent supercomputational programme developed by technology giant IBM and promoted to oncologists. Their campaign to introduce the programme to cancer specialists became a public-relations disaster. The AI purported to deliver high-quality treatment recommendations for 12 cancer types that collectively accounted for 80% of global cases. However, when clinicians first engaged with Watson they encountered an epistemic quandary that complicated its clinical uptake. When Watson's guidance merely corroborated physicians' preexisting opinions, its recommendations were perceived as redundant and offered little practical value. The supercomputer thus affirmed what clinicians already knew, and its recommendations rarely altered therapeutic choices.

Conversely, when Watson issued recommendations at odds with expert consensus, clinicians tended to judge the system as lacking competence. Compounding this, the machine could not render accessible explanations of why its recommendations were plausible, because the opacity of its machine-learning algorithms precluded full human comprehension. As a result, suspicion and incredulity intensified, causing many physicians to dismiss seemingly unorthodox AI recommendations and default to their clinical expertise.

29

CThis instance is emblematic of a wider public lack of confidence in AI and a marked reluctance to embrace its purported advantages. Trust in others often emerges from psychological inferences about their thought processes combined with empirical experience of their reliability. Those dynamics engender a psychological sense of safety. AI, by contrast, remains relatively novel and unfamiliar to large swathes of the population. Even where technical explication is possible (and such clarity is not always attainable), the decision-making apparatus of AI tends to be too abstruse for general comprehension. Interacting with systems we do not understand can provoke anxiety and a pervasive sense of diminished control.

Many individuals also lack direct familiarity with numerous instances in which AI functions effectively, precisely because such operations typically occur behind the scenes. Instead, what captures public attention are conspicuous instances where AI systems malfunction or err. Embarrassing failures of AI attract disproportionate media coverage, reinforcing the narrative that technology cannot be unequivocally relied upon. Machine learning is inherently fallible, partly because its human designers are themselves imperfect and subject to bias.

30

DEmotional dispositions regarding AI run deep and are resistant to facile revision. In a recent experiment, participants drawn from a spectrum of backgrounds viewed a variety of science-fiction films about AI and were then interrogated about their attitudes to routine automation. The investigators found that mere exposure to cinematic visions of technological futures polarised participants' attitudes regardless of whether the portrayal of AI was benevolent or malign. Optimists became more emphatically enthusiastic while sceptics hardened into more guarded positions.

This outcome suggests that individuals filter and interpret evidence about AI in ways that bolster preexisting attitudes—a manifestation of the entrenched cognitive tendency known as 'confirmation bias'. As portrayals of AI proliferate across media and entertainment, they may contribute to a societal schism between beneficiaries of AI and those who reject its incorporation. More pertinently, eschewing the advantages enabled by AI could consign sizeable segments of the populace to substantive disadvantage.

31

EFortunately, a number of promising strategies for enhancing trust in AI are already apparent. Prior experiential contact with AI significantly ameliorates public opinion of the technology, as the aforementioned study indicates. Evidence further suggests that increased habitual engagement with technologies such as the internet correlates with heightened trust in those technologies.

Another plausible remedy involves enhanced disclosure about the algorithms that underpin AI and the objectives they are programmed to achieve. Several high-profile social media companies and online marketplaces already publish transparency reports documenting government requests and surveillance disclosures. Applying a comparable transparency regime to AI could cultivate a more sophisticated public understanding of how algorithmic decisions are constituted.

32

FResearch suggests that granting users calibrated control over AI decision-making can bolster trust and enable systems to assimilate human experiential knowledge. For example, one study demonstrated that when individuals were permitted to make minor adjustments to an algorithm, they reported greater satisfaction with its outputs, a stronger inclination to regard it as superior, and an increased propensity to reuse it.

Although intimate technical understanding of AI's intricate inner workings is not required, conferring a degree of responsibility over implementation to end-users materially increases their willingness to incorporate AI into their daily lives.
